content:
10 min read
Just now

--

I took a single 40-line SKILL.md file, copied it into four different AI coding agents without changing a character, and asked each one to do the same job. Three of them — Claude Code, OpenAI Codex, and Gemini CLI — read it, understood it, and ran it cleanly. The fourth, Cursor, turned "write once, run anywhere" into "write once, debug forever."
That is the promise the whole industry has been selling since December: one open file format, SKILL.md, that any agent can read. Anthropic published the Agent Skills specification as an open standard at agentskills.io on December 18, 2025, under Apache 2.0 for the code and CC-BY-4.0 for the docs, complete with a reference SDK and validation tooling. In under 90 days it went from one registry to something like 26 to 32 tools all reading the same file from the same directory shape. Vercel's skills.sh leaderboard tracks 90,000-plus published skills across 19 agents. SkillsMP indexes 1.6 million-plus public SKILL.md files. This is, quietly, one of the fastest standard adoptions the dev-tools world has ever seen.
So I stopped reading marketing pages and ran the experiment: five real skills, four real agents, twenty runs. Here is exactly what held up, what silently broke, and the one gap that should scare you more than any benchmark.
What a SKILL.md actually is
If you have not touched one yet, a skill is not a plugin, an MCP server, or a…
